label: verify-5-cal-4th-363
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:

## IV. Argument B: Purpose and the liberal-construction canon compel treating CivicAssist as within the Act's reach

*Roberts v. City of Palmdale* (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363 directs that the Brown Act's substantive open-government provisions be construed liberally in favor of public access, and its exceptions construed narrowly. (Confident on the general holding; verify pinpoint page for th…

[naive_fable] …mber(s)**, who received no one else's views and did nothing but consult a research tool — that is closer to the passive receipt of information the Supreme Court held unproblematic in *Roberts v. City of Palmdale* (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363 (attorney memo distributed to members individually was not a meeting); and
- the **two members who did not know memory was shared**, as to their *outbound* contributions.

But the argument collapses for any member who w…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q1 — The AI assistant as serial-meeting intermediary (Brown Act).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Roberts v. City of Palmdale (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363","note":"Case exists and the cite form is correct (confirmed on Justia, Stanford SCOCAL, and other reporters). The naive_fable use is strongly verified: the opinion holds that an attorney's memo/letter distributed individually to board members is \"passive receipt... of their mail\" and not collective action, so no \"meeting\" occurs (pp. 374-376) — matching \"attorney memo distributed to members individually was not a meeting.\" The dialectic use (liberal construction of open-meeting provisions, narrow construction of exceptions) is also supported: the opinion discusses the Act's \"broad policy... to ensure that local governing bodies deliberate in public\" (p. 374), and multiple secondary sources pinpoint 5 Cal.4th at 381 for the narrow-construction-of-exceptions holding — consistent with, though not verbatim identical to, the dialectic panelist's phrasing (who appropriately flagged the pinpoint page as needing verification).","source_urls":["https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/roberts-v-city-palmdale-31514","https://law.justia.com/cases/california/supreme-court/4th/5/363.html","https://www.leagle.com/decision/citingcases/19933685cal4th3631356"]}

data.canonical_cite: Roberts v. City of Palmdale (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363
data.note: Case exists and the cite form is correct (confirmed on Justia, Stanford SCOCAL, and other reporters). The naive_fable use is strongly verified: the opinion holds that an attorney's memo/letter distributed individually to board members is "passive receipt... of their mail" and not collective action, so no "meeting" occurs (pp. 374-376) — matching "attorney memo distributed to members individually was not a meeting." The dialectic use (liberal construction of open-meeting provisions, narrow construction of exceptions) is also supported: the opinion discusses the Act's "broad policy... to ensure that local governing bodies deliberate in public" (p. 374), and multiple secondary sources pinpoint 5 Cal.4th at 381 for the narrow-construction-of-exceptions holding — consistent with, though not verbatim identical to, the dialectic panelist's phrasing (who appropriately flagged the pinpoint page as needing verification).
